summary: Re-verify the 40 prior review cards adversarially + sweep for new gaps, return consolidated data

verified.oneLine: TerminalBoard does not receive fullView from BoardNode (actual line 213, not 184), so its LOD card at line 706 unconditionally paints over the live xterm when camera zooms out during full view.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the LOD card wrapper (line 707) has `pointerEvents: none`, meaning user clicks still pass through to the xterm underneath — so the terminal remains technically interactive even when visually obscured. One could argue this reduces the defect to cosmetic. However, this argument does not hold: the LOD BoardFrame renders with an opaque `--surface` background fill that completely hides the terminal screen content from the user, even though pointer events pass through. The user cannot see the running session output, making this a genuine user-facing defect rather than merely cosmetic. The PTY session itself is unaffected (no data loss or crash), which is why the severity is Medium and not High.

verified.oneLine: fullViewId is still not cleared synchronously on the key-delete (onNodesChange remove) and undo/redo paths, leaving a one-render stale window; the hardCloseFullView helper exists and is used on the menu-delete path but is absent from both other delete vectors.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug is that the deferred useEffect at line 430 (setFullViewId guard on [boards]) has been expanded since the card was written to also clear fullViewId — not just focusedId — so the stale window is self-healing within the same React commit flush. Additionally, fullViewBoard (line 531) is derived via boards.find(), so it becomes undefined immediately in the same render that removes the board, and FullViewModal renders on fullViewBoard rather than fullViewId directly, so the modal closes visually before the stale fullViewId is even observed. This means the visible UI impact is nil. However, this refutation does NOT hold: the BrowserPreviewLayer rAF loop (lines 667-687) guards on fullViewId state (not fullViewBoard), the capture-phase Escape handler (line 483) also guards on fullViewId state, and both fire in the stale render pass. The rAF loop calls geomRef.current.get(fullViewId) for a board that no longer exists, and the Escape handler would respond to Esc by calling closeFullView() (triggering an exit animation for a non-existent board). The pattern asymmetry is explicit: boardActions.remove (line 392) correctly calls hardCloseFullView() when fullViewIdRef.current === id, but the onNodesChange remove branch (line 289) and doUndo/doRedo (lines 414, 419) only call setFocusedId with no matching fullViewId clear. The finding is valid despite the line numbers having shifted.

verified.oneLine: The "full view stays open on Esc" half of the bug is already fixed by a capture-phase window handler added to Canvas.tsx (lines 481-491); the residual is that the BoardMenu's local `open` state and previewStore `menuOpen` flag are never reset by that same Esc because stopPropagation in the capture handler prevents the menu's document-level bubble handler from firing.
verified.refutation: Canvas.tsx lines 474-491 add a window-level capture-phase keydown listener (`addEventListener('keydown', onEscapeCapture, true)`) that explicitly fires BEFORE xterm's bubble-phase `stopKeys`, calls `closeFullView()`, and even has a comment stating "xterm calls stopPropagation() on keydown, so a bubble-phase listener never sees Esc from a focused full-view terminal." This directly and fully resolves the primary claim — "neither the full view nor the menu close" — because the full view now always exits. Additionally, the finding's Canvas.tsx line references (307-311) are completely wrong: that range is the `addCentered` callback, not a keydown handler at all, meaning the card was written against an older version of the file. The remaining sub-issue (menuOpen stuck true) is real but narrower in impact: the menu popover is visually inside the full-view modal that is now animating closed, so the user does not see a stranded menu on the canvas. The `menuOpen` flag in previewStore does stay true until the user next interacts (a subsequent pointerdown on the canvas would fire the `document.addEventListener('pointerdown', close)` handler in BoardMenu, clearing it). So the "permanent session-long detach" described in the original finding does not hold; it self-heals on any subsequent pointer interaction.

verified.oneLine: The useEffect unconditionally calls setMenuOpen(open) on every render (lines 170-173, not 108-111 as cited), causing a redundant false→false call and a double false-call on menu close, but Zustand's built-in equality guard in previewStore.ts:119 absorbs both without any observable side-effect.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is entirely harmless by design: previewStore.ts:119 implements setMenuOpen as `set((s) => (s.menuOpen === active ? s : { menuOpen: active }))`, returning the same state reference when the value is unchanged. Zustand's Object.is check on the returned state then suppresses all subscriber notifications, so no re-renders, no detach/reattach cycles, and no downstream effects occur. The same guard pattern is used for setNodeGesture (line 118), indicating intentional defensive design. This argument does not defeat the finding — the double-call and unconditional body call are real, code is exactly as described — but it fully explains why the finding's own summary correctly says "no observable mis-render or detach/reattach cycle". The finding is confirmed as a pure code-quality/style issue with no functional consequence.

verified.oneLine: The RF delete-key and undo/redo remove paths never call setFullViewId(null)/hardCloseFullView before removeBoard, leaving fullViewIdRef.current stale in BrowserPreviewLayer's synchronous Zustand subscriber, which then closes all other live Browser renderers via the full-view branch of applyLiveness.
verified.refutation: The strongest refutation is that the boardActions.remove path (line 392 in Canvas.tsx) DOES call `hardCloseFullView()` before `removeBoard(id)`, and one could argue the same pattern should work for the RF delete and undo paths — perhaps the heal effect (line 430) fires quickly enough before any user action, or fullViewId is rarely set when these paths are triggered in practice. However, this argument fails: `hardCloseFullView()` calls `setFullViewId(null)`, which is a React state setter (batched/asynchronous), so even the boardActions.remove path does NOT synchronously update `fullViewIdRef.current` before `removeBoard` triggers the Zustand subscriber in BrowserPreviewLayer. The onNodesChange remove branch (line 282-290) and doUndo/doRedo (lines 411-420) are confirmed to have no `setFullViewId` or `hardCloseFullView` call whatsoever. The heal effect at line 430 is the sole mechanism for these paths and fires one React render after `removeBoard()`, while `fullViewIdRef.current` is only updated in `BrowserPreviewLayer`'s focusMounted effect (line 648), which runs after both the state update and a re-render — well after the synchronous Zustand subscriber at line 816 fires `applyLiveness()` with the stale ref. The bug is real.

verified.oneLine: The e2e setFullView hook passes the raw useState setter (setFullViewId) instead of openFullView, bypassing the already-live Slice 5 fullViewEntering motion flag and leaving the Browser-view hold-then-snap enter path uncovered by the harness.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the `FullViewModal` component always runs its own enter animation independently of `fullViewEntering` (FullViewModal.tsx lines 46-54: it unconditionally fires `requestAnimationFrame(() => setOpen(true))` on mount). So the visual modal animation is not broken. Furthermore, the `fullview-close` test (e2eSmoke.ts:336) does open the modal via the hook path and exercises the exit state machine (closeFullView / fullViewClosing) end-to-end, so the close path is partially covered. The argument fails, however, because `fullViewEntering` controls `fullViewMotion` (Canvas.tsx line 131), which BrowserPreviewLayer uses at lines 562 and 673 to hold a Browser native view detached during the enter tween to avoid snapping to a scale-polluted rect. When `setFullViewId` is called directly, `fullViewMotion` stays false, so the hold-then-snap is never triggered in any test — a regression there (e.g. the view attaching prematurely to a transform-in-progress rect) would not be caught. The finding is confirmed.

verified.oneLine: requestReload on a deleted board id writes a phantom previewStore.byId entry that the reconcile loop never reclaims, confirmed at the cited lines with no guard present.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that selectBoard(deletedId) at Canvas.tsx:372 does change canvasStore (sets selectedId), which fires the canvasStore subscriber and triggers reconcile — so reconcile DOES run shortly after the phantom entry is created. However, this does not refute the bug: reconcile's removal loop at BrowserPreviewLayer.tsx:698-704 iterates recs.current.keys(), not previewStore.byId keys. The deleted board's id was removed from recs.current when the board was deleted (via the earlier reconcile triggered by removeBoard), so the phantom byId entry is invisible to the cleanup loop even on the subsequent reconcile runs. There is no previewStore subscriber anywhere in the codebase (grep confirms zero hits for previewStore.subscribe / usePreviewStore.subscribe), and no background sweep over byId keys. The counter-argument does not hold.

verified.oneLine: onPreview lacks setPortChoices(null)/setBrowserPick(null) resets at entry, allowing both port-choice and browser-pick overlays to render simultaneously on a rapid globe re-press.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the scenario requires a very precise sequence: the user must re-trigger the globe while a picker is already open and before dismissing it, AND the second detectPorts call must return a result that leads to the other picker being opened (e.g., browserPick already open, then >=2 URLs returned to open portChoices). In practice this is a narrow, user-driven race. Furthermore, React's async state batching may partially mitigate the visual overlap if both setState calls land in the same render cycle. The overlays both have visible Cancel buttons so the user can recover without data loss. This argument does NOT hold strongly enough to call it a false positive — the code genuinely has no mutual-exclusion guard, the render conditions at lines 608 and 629 are independent booleans, and the async await at line 527 creates a real window where stale state persists while a new flow begins. The bug is real, though correctly classified as Info/cosmetic.

verified.oneLine: flush() is void and before-quit never awaits a pending project:save IPC, so the last ~1 s of edits can be silently lost on quit
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the `blur` + `beforeunload` flush provides a second save opportunity before the window fully closes, and `writeFileAtomic` (via the `.bak` rotation in `writeProject`) ensures that any write that _does_ complete is fully atomic — so there is never partial corruption, only a potential missed write. Additionally, Electron's `before-quit` handler currently awaits `shutdown()` (~2 s PTY drain) which gives the in-flight IPC some time to resolve in practice. However, this does not hold: `shutdown()` explicitly only drains PTYs and previews (lines 196-201 of `index.ts`); it has no reference to any pending `project:save` IPC. `flush()` is typed `(): void` (line 19 of `useAutosave.ts`), `run()` does `void opts.save()` (line 33), and the React cleanup callback at line 82 also calls `saver.flush()` fire-and-forget. No `flushSave` channel, `savePending` flag, or save-drain IPC exists anywhere in the codebase. The race described in the finding is structurally present in the current code.

verified.oneLine: touchRecent uses non-atomic writeFileSync for recent-projects.json while write-file-atomic is already imported in the sibling projectStore.ts and declared as a runtime dependency.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that recent-projects.json is a pure UX convenience cache, not user project data. Corruption silently resets the MRU list to empty; the user loses no actual work and can reopen projects manually. writeFileSync is synchronous and issues a single OS write call — on most modern operating systems the kernel page-cache means a partial write from a crash would require actual power loss or SIGKILL during the microsecond-scale syscall, making the practical trigger window extremely narrow for a desktop Electron app. This argument does not hold completely: the condition is real, the crash window exists on every project open, the silent recovery-less catch at line 36 is confirmed in the code, and since the fix is a one-line import swap with zero risk it should still be applied — but it does justify keeping severity at Low rather than elevating it.

verified.oneLine: assertBoard runs on pre-migration board data at line 381, before migrate() at line 402, which is harmless today but will break loading of older-version docs the moment any future migration reshapes a board field.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: the only migration in MIGRATIONS (line 207-210) is the v1-to-v2 step, which exclusively adds a `viewport` key to the document root and does not touch any board fields at all. Therefore, assertBoard at line 381 sees exactly the same board data it would see post-migration — the ordering flaw produces no incorrect behavior for any document version that currently exists. The bug is entirely latent: it requires a future developer to (a) add a new migration that modifies board fields AND (b) fail to notice that assertBoard is called before migrate(). This is a code-quality / maintainability concern, not an active defect. However, the ordering flaw is real and structurally present in the code exactly as described, so the risk is genuine and the finding cannot be dismissed as a false positive — it is correctly labeled a latent bug, confirmed at Low severity.

verified.oneLine: writeProject (projectStore.ts:62-73) rotates .bak before validating the incoming doc, so a single malformed write destroys both files; isEnvelope exists (line 33-40) but is applied only on the read path.
verified.refutation: All production callers of project:save (useAutosave.ts:57, AppChrome.tsx:59) invoke useCanvasStore.getState().toObject(), which delegates to boardSchema.toObject() (boardSchema.ts:196-202). That function always returns { schemaVersion: SCHEMA_VERSION, viewport: ..., boards: structuredClone(boards) } — a structurally valid envelope by construction. No current code path can pass a non-envelope to writeProject through the normal renderer flow. The IPC foreign-sender guard (projectIpc.ts:19-22, 30) also blocks cross-origin injection. The gap is real as a missing defensive check on a public export, but it is not exploitable with today's callers, so severity is Low (edge/defensive) rather than the card's Info — the card actually understates the potential severity while being right that it is not currently triggering.

verified.oneLine: The 'existing' branch of applyPush (Canvas.tsx:365-372) calls st.updateBoard() without a preceding st.beginChange(), silently destroying any armed redo branch and leaving the push non-undoable.
verified.refutation: The strongest counterargument is that a globe-push is an intentional, non-reversible navigation action — one could argue it should not be undoable and clearing redo is expected behavior. This does not hold: (a) the peer path in BrowserBoard.tsx:142 explicitly calls beginChange() before updateBoard, establishing the project's own convention that URL mutations are undoable checkpoints; (b) the spawn path (addBoard) does record an undo checkpoint, creating an asymmetry between the two branches of the same action; (c) the user has no indication their redo history was erased, making this a silent data-integrity issue rather than a design choice. The refutation fails.

verified.oneLine: borderPoint at line 31 produces NaN scale when a node has not yet been measured (w/h = 0 via ?? 0 fallback) and the two node centers are axis-aligned (dy === 0 or dx === 0), causing a malformed SVG path for the first render frame.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that when both dimensions are zero (sx=0 AND sy=0) and neither dx nor dy is zero (non-axis-aligned case), the formula gives Math.max(Infinity, Infinity) = Infinity, then 1/Infinity = 0, and scale = 0, so the returned point is just the node center — not NaN, just a degenerate zero-length edge. This means NaN only manifests in the specific axis-aligned sub-case where exactly one of dx or dy is zero while the corresponding half-dimension is also zero. However, this counter-argument does not hold for the stated scenario: when dy === 0 and sy === 0, JavaScript evaluates 0/0 = NaN and Math.max(finite-or-Infinity, NaN) = NaN per the IEEE 754 spec that JS follows, so scale = NaN and the path is malformed. The axis-aligned placement of Terminal/Browser boards on a canvas grid makes this sub-case plausible at startup. No additional guard exists in the file or in any calling code to prevent borderPoint from receiving zero-dimension boxes.

verified.oneLine: parsePortsFromOutput always appends an explicit :port suffix even when the port is the scheme default (80/443), producing non-canonical URLs like http://localhost:80 from bare-hostname input.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: (1) the card itself concedes this is "near-never triggered in practice" because real dev servers always print an explicit port; (2) the produced URL http://localhost:80 is functionally 100% identical to http://localhost — browsers, HTTP clients, and RFC 7230 all treat them equivalently, so no navigation, CORS, or display behavior changes; (3) the port field on the returned DetectedUrl object is correctly set to 80/443, which is the only value downstream consumers (e.g., deduplication at line 36) actually use for logic. The issue cannot cause a crash, data loss, or security exploit. This refutation does not fully hold because the non-canonical form could still confuse users who copy the stored URL manually, making it a legitimate cosmetic/code-quality nit — but severity Info (not Medium or higher) is correct.

verified.oneLine: The CSI-only ANSI regex at portDetect.ts:13 passes OSC sequences through, causing a potential double-URL match in the internal `found` array, but the dedup Map at lines 34-38 absorbs both and the public API output is always correct.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: the deduplication Map (lines 34-38 of portDetect.ts) is explicitly designed to merge multiple appearances of the same host:port, keying on `${f.host}:${f.port}` and keeping the highest-index occurrence. The `found` array is an internal implementation detail never directly exposed to callers. The public return value of `parsePortsFromOutput` is always correctly deduplicated regardless of whether OSC sequences cause double matches. The finding itself acknowledges "there is no functional regression." This argument is strong but does not fully nullify the issue: the internal `found` array IS noisier than intended, the gap IS untested, and a future refactor that removes or bypasses dedup would silently regress. The finding is technically accurate as a code-quality/defensive concern, which aligns with its Info severity.

verified.oneLine: The demoting set is populated at line 497 before the capturePreview await but is only cleared at line 537 on the happy path; the early-return at line 505 when the gesture ends mid-capture leaves stale IDs that permanently suppress flushBatch for those boards.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that a subsequent beginMotion cycle re-adds the stale IDs and eventually reaches the delete at line 537 (if the next gesture completes normally), so the condition is self-healing. This argument does not hold: any gesture that ends before capturePreview resolves reproduces the stale entries, and the user can go an entire session without triggering a second full-round-trip gesture cycle, leaving affected boards permanently misaligned. The flushBatch skip at line 456 has no TTL or fallback — it is unconditional.

verified.oneLine: The stale-owner scenario is structurally impossible: disposeAll() empties both `owner` and `views` atomically, so preview:attach can never reach attach() with a null owner.
verified.refutation: The finding's premise is that `preview:attach` can call `attach()` when `owner` is null (e.g., after `disposeAll()`). In the current code, `disposeAll()` (lines 509-511) calls `disposeOne(id)` for every key in `views`, and `disposeOne()` (lines 318-328) calls `views.delete(id)` at line 327. After `disposeAll()` finishes, both `owner` is null AND `views` is empty. The `preview:attach` handler (lines 435-440) guards with `const e = views.get(args.id)` followed by `if (e) attach(...)` — when `views` is empty, `e` is undefined and `attach()` is never invoked. The invariant that `owner` is non-null whenever any entry exists in `views` is enforced structurally: `ensure()` is the only writer of both `owner` (line 168) and new entries in `views` (line 184), so a non-null entry in `views` implies `owner` was non-null at entry-creation time. The only way `owner` can become null afterward is via `disposeAll()`, which simultaneously empties `views`. Therefore the null-owner code path in `attach()` (line 294) is unreachable through the `preview:attach` IPC handler under any normal or reordering scenario. Additionally, all line numbers cited in the finding are off by approximately 10-11 lines compared to current code (e.g., `attach` is at line 293, not 282; `preview:attach` handler is at line 435, not 423; `disposeAll` is at line 509, not 499), indicating the finding was authored against a different version of the file. The structural soundness argument in the finding is theoretical and does not apply to the current implementation.

verified.oneLine: The `as string` widening and stale comment for `did-start-navigation` in BrowserPreviewLayer.tsx are real but the cited line numbers (779-781, 784) are wrong — the actual code is at lines 855-860.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: the `as string` widening is an intentional defensive pattern applied uniformly across the same handler — line 849 applies `(ev.type as string) === 'escape'` because `escape` is genuinely absent from the preload PreviewEvent union (it exists only in src/main/preview.ts:20). The pattern was introduced to handle out-of-union event types forwarded over IPC. When `did-start-navigation` was later added to the preload union (src/preload/index.ts:53), the renderer was not updated, leaving an unnecessary cast for that one case. The cast is harmless at runtime and the inconsistency is cosmetic — removing it would be purely a type-system improvement with no observable behavior change. This refutation does not hold fully because the comment is factually incorrect (it asserts the union omits the variant when it does not), which is a genuine maintenance hazard.

verified.oneLine: closeBoard (lines 437-448) does not reset lastZoom, leaving a stale value that is currently harmless because attachBoard always overwrites it before any IPC, but the implicit invariant is fragile.
verified.refutation: The strongest refutation is that this is unreachable as a real bug today: the diff-skip early-return in attachBoard (lines 399-407) requires both r.exists && r.attached to be true, but closeBoard sets both to false (lines 440-441). This means every re-attach path after a closeBoard unconditionally falls through to line 410 (r.lastZoom = zoomFactor) before any IPC is issued. There is no current code path that reads r.lastZoom on a closed record without first writing a fresh value. This refutation holds completely for the current codebase — the finding is latent risk only, not an active defect.

verified.oneLine: launch()'s .then()/.catch() callbacks inside spawn() lack a disposed/termRef staleness guard, unlike the parallel respawn() and adoptTerminal paths, though the practical impact is nil in React 18 + xterm's no-op write-after-dispose.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: (1) In React 18.3.1 (confirmed version), calling a useState setter after component unmount is silently dropped — React 18 explicitly removed the "Can't perform a setState on an unmounted component" warning, so there are no runtime warnings. (2) Calling term.write() on a disposed xterm Terminal is a verified silent no-op — tested directly against node_modules/@xterm/xterm/lib/xterm.js, no exception is thrown. (3) The only actionable code path inside .then() (line 358) is gated on res.state === 'spawn-failed', which is an already-rare error condition, making the window of exposure (unmount racing a failed spawn) extremely narrow. These three factors together mean the missing guard produces no crash, no data loss, no user-visible warning, and no stuck UI state — making this a pure defensive-coding gap rather than a real defect. This argument does not fully hold for the .catch() path (line 363-366), which has no guard and fires on any IPC rejection, but the consequences remain the same no-op outcomes.

verified.oneLine: Restored terminal boards unconditionally spawn a shell on project open, violating the CLAUDE.md contract that states "restored terminals are idle (no auto-spawn)".
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that CLAUDE.md's "restored terminals are idle" sentence could be aspirational/planned documentation rather than a description of implemented behavior. However, that section of CLAUDE.md reads as a retrospective summary of what Phase 3 Slice A built ("built on branch phase-3-persistence...restored terminals are idle"), not a forward spec — so the contract is presented as delivered. Regardless, the actual behavior (auto-spawn on every restore) is clearly undesirable from a UX standpoint even without the documented contract, since every terminal the user has ever saved will activate automatically on project open without any interaction. This refutation does not hold.

verified.oneLine: Parked PTY sessions are not reaped on project switch because disposeLiveResources only kills sessions in the live sessions map and no IPC path exists to reach the private reapParked function.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the bug requires a specific two-step sequence: the user must delete a terminal board (triggering park) AND then switch projects within the 120-second TTL window. If the user switches projects without first deleting the terminal board, disposeLiveResources correctly kills the live session via cleanup(id). This means the parked-session leak only occurs in the narrow delete-then-switch-quickly scenario, not every project switch. However, this refutation does not hold — with long-running agentic CLI sessions (the primary use-case for this app) the scenario is entirely realistic, and the code gap is real: a search across all of src/ confirms reapParked (pty.ts:83) is module-private with zero IPC exposure, cleanup (pty.ts:453-465) short-circuits at line 455 if the id is absent from sessions without touching parked, and preload/index.ts has no reapParkedTerminals or killAllTerminals entry. The bug is exactly as described.

verified.oneLine: Unix killTree branch returns Promise.resolve() immediately after SIGKILL, breaking the JSDoc-documented awaitable drain guarantee that only the Windows taskkill branch actually fulfills.
verified.refutation: SIGKILL cannot be caught, blocked, or deferred by user-mode code. The kernel delivers it to all processes in the group (kill(-pid, SIGKILL) targets the whole process group) near-instantaneously, so in practice app.exit fires after the processes are already dead. The only realistic orphan scenario requires a grandchild that has called setsid()/setpgrp() to escape the process group — typical for explicit daemon-style spawns but not for normal compiler or agent subprocess trees. The microtask queue drain between Promise.resolve() returning and app.exit(0) executing provides additional (if unguaranteed) margin. This makes the documented contract mismatch a real correctness gap but an essentially theoretical one in the shipping use case (Windows is the primary target, and macOS/Linux users would need an escaping grandchild subprocess to observe orphaning).

verified.oneLine: The onWinMsg MessagePort handoff accepts ports based on a UUID match with no e.origin check, but this is defense-in-depth only: sandbox+contextIsolation prevent any untrusted script from reaching the renderer, and the wildcard origin used by the preload makes an origin check ineffective anyway.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is not a real bug at all — it is an acknowledged architectural characteristic with zero exploitable threat surface. The Electron app enforces `sandbox: true` and `contextIsolation: true` (confirmed at src/main/index.ts:45-46), which means no untrusted JavaScript can ever reach the renderer world to post a spoofed handoff message. Furthermore, the finding card itself admits that the obvious remediation (adding an `e.origin` check) would be useless because the preload posts with wildcard `'*'`, so `e.origin` is not a reliable discriminator. The UUID used as the discriminator is a 128-bit random value generated by the canvas store, making a collision attack computationally infeasible. There is no code path by which an attacker can exploit this gap under the current or any plausible future configuration without first compromising the renderer process entirely — at which point the missing nonce provides no additional protection. This argument does not fully hold only to the extent that defense-in-depth nonces are still a reasonable hardening measure; however, the practical risk is genuinely zero.

verified.oneLine: The preload script forwards the PTY MessagePort with a wildcard targetOrigin ('*') and the renderer's onWinMsg handler accepts it with no e.origin check, violating defense-in-depth, though no current exploit path exists due to sandbox/contextIsolation and absence of cross-origin frames.
verified.refutation: The strongest counterargument is that the application's own architecture makes this unexploitable today: (1) webviewTag is explicitly false (src/main/index.ts:48), (2) no iframe or webview elements exist anywhere in the renderer source, (3) sandbox:true and contextIsolation:true are both enforced (src/main/index.ts:45-46), (4) WebContentsView previews run in entirely separate renderer processes that cannot receive window.postMessage from the main world, and (5) setWindowOpenHandler denies all new-window creation and will-frame-navigate is guarded. The finding itself concedes there is no exploit path today. However, this argument does not fully negate the issue — the pattern violation is genuine code, the wildcard at preload line 143 is confirmed present, and the missing origin guard at TerminalBoard.tsx line 307 is confirmed absent. The fix is trivially small and the pattern is real, so the finding is confirmed at Low severity rather than dismissed.

verified.oneLine: writeProject (projectStore.ts:62-73) unconditionally overwrites both canvas.json and canvas.json.bak with any doc value, because isEnvelope is never called on the incoming payload — only on the prior file to decide .bak rotation.
verified.refutation: The strongest refutation is that the normal application save path is fully typed and structurally sound: useAutosave.ts:57 calls window.api.project.save(useCanvasStore.getState().toObject()), and toObject() (boardSchema.ts:196-202) always returns a proper CanvasDoc with schemaVersion set to the SCHEMA_VERSION integer constant and boards as a structuredClone of a typed Board[]. Additionally, contextBridge + contextIsolation (preload/index.ts:146-151) means external scripts cannot call project:save, and the foreign-frame guard (projectIpc.ts:66) blocks sub-frames. In practice no code today passes a non-envelope to writeProject. This argument does not hold as a complete refutation, however, because the IPC boundary is untyped at runtime (doc: unknown), TypeScript gives no runtime guarantee, and any future renderer bug or additional code path calling window.api.project.save with a bad value — such as toObject returning an unexpected shape after a future store refactor — would irreversibly destroy both the live file and the only backup with no rejection or error.

verified.oneLine: Bare `ws:` wildcard in the DEV CSP is real, but the finding's key claim — that it ships in the production binary — is false; the cspMeta Vite plugin rewrites it to `connect-src 'self'` at build time.
verified.refutation: The finding's central severity driver is "the permissive meta-CSP also ships in the packaged binary." This is directly contradicted by the code. `electron.vite.config.ts` lines 27-29 define `PROD_CSP = "... connect-src 'self'"` (no `ws:` at all), and the `cspMeta` plugin (lines 31-43) chooses between `DEV_CSP` and `PROD_CSP` via `ctx.server ? DEV_CSP : PROD_CSP` — `ctx.server` is `undefined` during `pnpm build`, so PROD_CSP is injected. The actual built artifact `out/renderer/index.html` line 16 confirms: `connect-src 'self'`. End-users running the packaged app are never exposed to `ws:`. The residual issue is real only in `pnpm dev` and only if a supply-chain-compromised npm package is present on the developer's own machine — a threat model that is Local/Low severity, not user-facing. Additionally, the cited file:line reference is wrong: the finding says `src/renderer/index.html:13` but the `ws:` token is on line 16 of that file (inside a multi-line `<meta>` element starting at line 14; lines 6-13 are a comment block that itself explains the build-time rewrite).

verified.oneLine: The silent window.api fallback in the preload's else-branch (lines 148-151) exists exactly as described and would expose the full IPC API without error if contextIsolation were ever disabled, but the branch is unreachable in production because contextIsolation is hardcoded true in src/main/index.ts:46.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the else-branch is structurally unreachable without a deliberate source-code change and a full rebuild of the Electron app. contextIsolation: true is not a runtime config or environment variable — it is hardcoded at src/main/index.ts line 46 inside createWindow(), and the selfTest WebContentsView at selfTest.ts:92 also enforces the same posture. An attacker cannot flip this flag without modifying compiled output or source. The comment "should never happen" is accurate. There is no injection vector from the renderer that could reach this branch. The finding itself acknowledges the branch is unreachable in production. This is therefore a hardening recommendation (replace a permissive silent fallback with a loud throw) rather than an actively exploitable defect, which is why Low is the correct severity. The refutation does not hold as a full dismissal, however: the code path is live, takes a permissive action, and provides no observable failure signal, so the finding correctly identifies a real defensive-coding gap even if the immediate exploitation risk is negligible.

verified.oneLine: All element-mutation callbacks close over a stale render-time elements snapshot; a second mutation dispatched before re-render overwrites the first, with losses persisted by autosave.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that most user interactions are separated in time by at least one React re-render cycle, so the race window only opens when two store-mutating events are dispatched in the same browser task/microtask flush before React has committed the previous update. In React 18 concurrent mode, event-handler state updates are batched, which could reduce the frequency of re-render delays between events. However, this batching covers React's own useState/useReducer and does NOT protect Zustand's synchronous set; each call to updateBoard (lines 231-253 of canvasStore.ts) immediately and atomically lands in the store, making the intermediate state visible — but the callbacks' pre-computed arrays are already computed from the stale closure snapshot before the first commit lands. The functional set callback in Zustand receives fresh s.boards, but the `patch` passed in (e.g. { elements: next }) was already calculated from the old elements at line 138. The refutation therefore does not hold: the race is real whenever two separate browser events (textarea onChange + checkbox click) fire close enough together that React has not yet re-rendered and updated the elements closure.

verified.oneLine: recordPast stores shared references but all current callers produce fresh arrays before storing, so no actual aliasing corruption exists today; the "bug" is a hypothetical future misuse scenario explicitly admitted in the card itself.
verified.refutation: The finding's own "How it triggers" section concedes the defect does not trigger with the current codebase. Reading the actual code confirms this is correct: every call site that passes `s.boards` to `recordPast` does so BEFORE producing the new boards array (addBoard line 179, removeBoard line 189, duplicateBoard line 223, beginChange line 297), so the reference stored in `past` is always the OLD array, and subsequent `.map` spreads in updateBoard (line 240) and resizeBoard (line 258) produce a structurally-distinct new array for `boards`. The two-phase pattern (beginChange snapshots old ref, then updateBoard/resizeBoard emit a new ref) provides exactly the aliasing safety claimed to be missing. Furthermore, any in-place Zustand state mutation is a fundamental Zustand anti-pattern that would break React rendering long before it could silently corrupt undo history — a developer writing `board.title = x` without returning a new state object from `set()` would see immediate render failures across the UI. The "undocumented contract" framing is cosmetically valid, but the actual risk of a silent violation is negligible. Severity is appropriately downgraded from Low to Info because there is zero user-visible defect and zero realistic exploit path in the current codebase.

verified.oneLine: After redo(), beginChange() always pushes a duplicate snapshot because the strict-reference guard (canvasStore.ts:288) compares past.last (pre-redo boards) against boards (redo-state boards), which are always different object references, causing an extra undo step to be required after redo+drag.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the redo-state boards object IS a genuinely new reference (future[0]) that was never in past after the redo, so pushing it is not truly a "duplicate" — the semantic content at past.last (x=0) differs from boards (x=200), meaning beginChange is correctly recording a new baseline. However, this does not hold: after the drag commits (updateBoard sets boards=x=300 and clears future), the past stack contains [..., x=0, x=200, x=200-snapshot-from-beginChange] — two consecutive entries referencing the x=200 state — so the first undo produces no visible change. The refutation fails because the phantom entry is semantically redundant regardless of reference distinctness.

verified.oneLine: The "two undo history entries" claim is false — only addBoard writes to past; updateBoard never calls recordPast, so a single Ctrl+Z correctly removes the whole push-to-preview operation.
verified.refutation: The finding's central claim — that the addBoard+updateBoard sequence writes two undo history entries — is refuted by the code. canvasStore.ts:178-183 shows addBoard unconditionally calls set() with past: recordPast(s.past, s.boards), writing exactly one checkpoint (the pre-add boards array). canvasStore.ts:231-253 shows updateBoard calls set() returning only { boards } or { boards, future: [] } — it never calls recordPast and never writes to past. selectBoard at line 282 writes only selectedId. After the three-call spawn sequence (Canvas.tsx:374-376, not the cited 248-249 which do not exist), the history stack has exactly one new past entry. history.ts:14-25 confirms applyUndo pops a single past entry and restores the full pre-add state. A user pressing Ctrl+Z once will see the board disappear entirely — the intermediate bare-board state is never stored in past and cannot be reached via undo. The card's own "Verification evidence" section contradicts the Summary: it admits the intermediate state is "unreachable via undo" and that only one past entry is written, while still asserting two undo steps are required. The line references (Canvas.tsx:248-249 for the spawn branch) are also wrong; the current code places applyPush at lines 358-378 with the spawn branch at 373-376. The residual concern — two render passes and a transient bare-board flash that may tickle autosave debounce — is real but cosmetic and does not constitute a user-visible undo bug as described.

verified.oneLine: Every checklist checkbox tick calls beginChange() and pushes a discrete Ctrl+Z step, making each toggle individually undoable — a design choice that is neither documented as intentional nor as a known trade-off.
verified.refutation: DESIGN.md §7.3 line 228 says "Toggling is live" and the JSDoc on toggleItem (elements.ts:152) says "live progress" — both hint the operation was conceived as live state change, not a structural edit, which could argue for the current undo-tracked behavior being correct by design rather than an oversight. Additionally, the beginChange guard at canvasStore.ts:288 (skips if boards ref unchanged) prevents duplicate snapshots for back-to-back toggles within the same render cycle, slightly reducing the Ctrl+Z accumulation problem. The toggle being consistent with deleteEl and appendItem (also beginChange + commit) makes it part of the uniform edit model. None of these arguments fully refute the finding — they support the "full-history model" option the card itself presents, but the core claim stands: the design choice is undocumented and therefore ambiguous.

verified.oneLine: projectIpc.ts has no unit tests for its frame-guard, and isForeignSender is copy-pasted across three files with no shared source, creating regression risk but no active exploit.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is not a functional bug at all — all six handlers correctly call guard(e) today (confirmed at lines 33, 49, 58, 66, 74, 79), and the code comments in pty.ts (lines 282-285) explicitly note that preview WebContentsViews have no preload/ipcRenderer, making the gap "not exploitable" in the current architecture. CLAUDE.md line 197-198 itself labels this a "known minor gap" the team has consciously accepted. Because there is no live defect and no current exploitability, this is a test-coverage and maintainability concern, not a Medium-severity user-facing or security defect. The severity should be downgraded to Low. The verdict remains confirmed (the gap is real) but the finding overstates severity for a pre-existing, team-acknowledged missing-test situation.

verified.oneLine: previewStore.test.ts EXISTS but covers only requestReload; patchIfPresent, clear, setNodeGesture, setMenuOpen, and selectLiveCount remain untested — the file-absence claim is wrong but the coverage gap is real.
verified.refutation: The finding's central factual claim — "previewStore.test.ts does not exist" — is FALSE. The file exists at Z:/Canvas ADE/src/renderer/src/store/previewStore.test.ts (44 lines, confirmed by Glob and Read). It contains a full describe block with four passing tests for requestReload. The Glob evidence cited in the card ("returns only history.test.ts, canvasStore.test.ts, persistence.integration.test.ts, useAutosave.test.ts") was stale at the time of writing or incorrectly recorded; the file now exists. Additionally, all five line-number references cited in the card are wrong: patchIfPresent is at line 104-105 (not 91), clear is at lines 106-112 (not 92-98), setNodeGesture is at line 118 (not 99), setMenuOpen is at line 119 (not 100), and selectLiveCount is at lines 128-132 (not 109-113). However, the coverage gap claim is still partially true: none of the five named functions (patchIfPresent, clear, setNodeGesture, setMenuOpen, selectLiveCount) have any test coverage anywhere in the test suite, confirmed by Grep across all *.test.ts files. The severity should be downgraded: missing unit tests for pure Zustand state functions is a Low/Info issue — the functions are observable through integration/e2e paths and are simple one-liner guards, not complex algorithmic logic. No crash, data-loss, or security-exploitable path is currently broken; this is a coverage hygiene issue, not a live defect.

verified.oneLine: All seven hardcoded hex values in the welcome CSS block were replaced with canonical design tokens in Phase 4 commit abd7fa2 before this finding card was reviewed.
verified.refutation: The finding is entirely accurate in describing a real defect, but it is no longer present. Commit abd7fa2 ("Phase 4 — Design pass & polish, Slice 4: states pass — welcome tokens + terminal braille spinner") explicitly addressed this exact issue. The commit message states: "Welcome/project-picker (index.css): replaced the hardcoded colours flagged by the Slice 2 audit with design tokens (--void/--text/--err/--surface-raised/--border/--accent/--text-3)". The git diff for that commit shows every specific substitution TQ-3 recommends was applied: `var(--bg, #0a0a0b)` → `var(--void)` (current line 658); `#e6e6e6` → `var(--text)` (current line 659); `#ff6b6b` → `var(--err)` (current line 671); `#2a2a2e`/`#161618` → `var(--border)`/`var(--surface-raised)` (current lines 681-683); `#161618` hover → `var(--surface-overlay)` (current line 729); `#888` → `var(--text-3)` (current line 738). The file currently contains zero raw hex literals in or after the welcome block (grep confirmed). The finding's cited line numbers (584-642) no longer correspond to the welcome block — the block now lives at lines 649-742. The comment at line 649 even reads "tokenised in §B", confirming the fix is intentional and documented.

verified.oneLine: terminalRuntimeStore's setRunning idempotency guard, clear early-exit, and selectRunningIds selector have zero unit-test coverage despite gating preview-edge stale/live rendering.
verified.refutation: The strongest argument against this being a real bug: previewEdges.test.ts (line 32-36) already exercises the downstream consumer of selectRunningIds by passing a manually constructed Set<string> directly to previewEdges(), verifying that stale/live edge state works correctly end-to-end. If the selectRunningIds selector were to break, the canvas-level integration would also break and the previewEdges tests would catch the symptom. Additionally, the store logic itself (lines 19-34 of terminalRuntimeStore.ts) is straightforward — the idempotency guard is a one-liner comparison and the clear operation is a standard map-delete pattern with an early-exit guard. These are not complex enough to hide subtle bugs. However, this argument does not hold fully: previewEdges.test.ts bypasses selectRunningIds entirely (it passes a raw Set, never calling the store), so regressions in setRunning or clear would not be caught by any existing test.

verified.oneLine: enumerateShells() at pty.ts:227 has no direct unit test; all cited line references are accurate and the coverage gap is real.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that the core deduplication primitive — canonicalizeShellPath, called inside the add() closure at pty.ts:236 — IS tested with three dedicated cases in pty.test.ts lines 53-79, covering short-name resolution, key-collision collapse, and ENOENT fallback. This means the most complex per-entry logic has coverage. The orchestrating layer (label dedup via seenLabels Set at line 240, the no-shells fallback at line 266, and the found[0].default assignment at line 267) is not tested, but these are simple assignments, not algorithmic — the fallback is two lines and the default flag is a single assignment. Additionally, enumerateShells() is exercised on every app startup (it drives the shell picker), giving it continuous integration-level smoke-testing in practice. This argument does NOT hold fully: the label-deduplication guard (seenLabels) and the ordering/default behavior are not observable through runtime smoke-testing alone, and any silent regression there would produce wrong UI state without a test failure to catch it. The finding stands.

verified.oneLine: Neither localServer.ts nor selfTest.ts has any test file; all cited line references (localServer.ts:57, 67-68; selfTest.ts:40-51, 94-106) match exactly, and a codebase-wide grep confirms zero test-file imports of these modules.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is purely a missing-test gap, not a functional defect. The `onError`/`server.once('error', onError)` wiring on lines 67-68 of localServer.ts is correct and complete as written, and the `finish`/`done` idempotency guards on selfTest.ts lines 40-51 and 94-106 correctly prevent double-resolve. No currently-shipped code path is broken. The risk is entirely prospective (a future refactor could regress these paths silently), not a present defect. Furthermore, selfTest.ts imports `WebContentsView` from Electron directly on line 2, making `testPreview` non-trivially testable in the plain Node/Vitest environment described in the suggested fix — a real test for that path would require heavier mocking or an Electron test harness that the project does not currently have. However, `localServer.ts` has no Electron dependency at all and is straightforwardly testable, and the `testPty` finish/done guard in selfTest.ts is pure logic that could be tested in isolation. The coverage gap is real and the finding's severity (Low) is calibrated correctly: no user-facing defect exists today, only unguarded regression risk.

verified.oneLine: ChecklistCard.test.tsx contains exactly one test (the drag-guard regression), leaving progress-bar, toggle, delete, and add-item event wiring with zero component-layer coverage.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is not a runtime defect at all — no user-facing behaviour is broken. The pure-function equivalents (checklistProgress, toggleItem, etc.) are already tested in elements.test.ts (lines 180-239), and those functions implement the same logic that ChecklistCard.tsx inlines at lines 92-94. Because the component merely calls onToggle/onDelete/onAddItem with the correct IDs (wiring that is visually obvious from reading the code), the risk of a silent regression is real but low, and only materialises if a future refactor breaks the wiring without a test to catch it. The finding is a test-quality gap, not a bug that has ever fired. Severity Info is correct and unchanged.

verified.oneLine: DiagOverlay.tsx is the sole file in spike/ but is permanently integrated into Canvas.tsx, FlowSmoke.tsx, and previewPlan.ts geometry — the misleading directory name risks accidental deletion.
verified.refutation: The strongest counter-argument is that this is purely speculative future harm: no runtime defect exists today. The import paths resolve correctly, the component compiles, and the DEV overlay functions as intended. A developer who deleted the file would immediately see a build error in Canvas.tsx and FlowSmoke.tsx (missing module), making silent breakage impossible — the deletion would be caught at compile time, not silently. The previewPlan.ts exclusion zone comment (line 124-125) explicitly names DiagOverlay.tsx, giving any developer who reads the file a clear signal the component is load-bearing. This refutation does not fully hold because (a) the spike/ directory name still implies "safe to delete" before reading previewPlan.ts, and (b) the geometric coupling in chromeExclusionZones (trW=270, trH=104) would not cause a build error if DiagOverlay were simply removed — only a silent layout regression in the native-view occlusion math.

newFindings.title: attachBoard post-openPreview guard only checks rec deletion, allows stale live:true after concurrent closeBoard
newFindings.summary: The guard after `await window.api.openPreview(...)` at line 429 checks `recs.current.has(g.id)` (deleted-board case) but NOT `r.exists` (closed-renderer case). If `closeBoard` runs during the IPC round-trip (e.g. the user enters full-view on another board, which calls `closeBoard` on all non-full-view renderers via `applyLiveness`), `r.exists` is reset to `false` but the rec remains in the map. The code falls through and calls `patchRuntime(g.id, { live: true })` on a board with no native renderer, inflating the DiagOverlay live count until the next `applyLiveness` or reconcile call resets it.

newFindings.title: boundsFor(g) called twice per board per frame: once explicitly, once inside zoomFor
newFindings.summary: `zoomFor(g)` is implemented as `fitZoomFactorForBounds(boundsFor(g).width, ...)` to preserve the Bug-#20 rounding invariant. In `flushBatch` (lines 463–464) and `reconcile` (lines 745–749), `boundsFor(g)` and `zoomFor(g)` are called separately. Because `zoomFor` internally calls `boundsFor`, the full `deviceStageRect → toWorldRect → worldRectToScreen → roundRect` pipeline plus `getViewport()` runs twice per board per `flushBatch` iteration and twice per board per store mutation in `reconcile`. With MAX_LIVE=4 boards at 60 Hz during camera motion, this doubles the geometry work on every frame.

newFindings.title: Full-view dedicated rAF loop runs every frame indefinitely with no idle-stop mechanism
newFindings.summary: The full-view rAF loop (lines 667–687) unconditionally re-queues itself every frame (`raf = requestAnimationFrame(tick)` is outside any condition), running at 60 Hz for the entire duration that `fullViewId` is set. Unlike the camera-driven pump which self-stops after 4 idle `flushBatch` frames, this loop has no idle-stop mechanism. Each tick issues a `document.querySelector` + `getBoundingClientRect()` + `host.contains()` DOM query even when the modal frame is stable and the view is attached at the correct position. The diff-skip in `flushBatch` prevents redundant IPC but the rAF overhead itself runs continuously.

newFindings.title: demoting set not cleared when endMotion fires before capture completes, causing native views to skip rAF pump position updates
newFindings.summary: In `beginMotion`, live boards are added to `demoting.current` before the async `capturePreview` batch. `demoting` is only cleared at line 537, inside the async IIFE, after `detachPreview` resolves. If `endMotion` fires while the captures are in flight (a short camera flick where the camera settles in ~16–33 ms while `capturePage` IPC takes ~10–50 ms), the early-return guard `if (!gestureRef.current) return` at line 505 aborts the IIFE without clearing `demoting`. The boards remain in `demoting.current` indefinitely. On subsequent camera gestures, `flushBatch` unconditionally skips every board in `demoting` (`if (demoting.current.has(g.id)) continue`), so the rAF pump never pushes position updates for those boards. The native views are visually stuck at the last `attachBoard`-computed rest position and do not follow camera panning until a longer gesture allows captures to complete before `endMotion` fires.

newFindings.title: stale overlap tints persist when dragged board is removed from store mid-drag
newFindings.summary: In the drag-snap pass of `onNodesChange`, `setGuides` and `setOverlaps` are only called when `boards.find((b) => b.id === single.id)` succeeds. If the dragged board disappears from the store mid-gesture (e.g. undo fires while dragging), neither update runs and the prior frame's guides and overlap tints stay visible until `onNodeDragStop` fires. The `else if (active.length > 0)` branch at line 224 only covers the multi-select/suppressed case, not the `single !== null && dragged === undefined` case.

newFindings.title: resize-end cleanup paths never call setOverlaps
newFindings.summary: All three resize-guide cleanup branches in `onNodesChange` (active-resize snap at line 263, resize-suppressed at lines 265–267, resize-settled at lines 268–272) call `setGuides` but never call `setOverlaps`. If overlap tints are somehow present when a resize starts (e.g. drag was interrupted without a clean drag-stop), they outlive the entire resize gesture because `onNodeDragStop` is never fired for a resize gesture. The fix is to mirror the `onNodeDragStop` pattern and call `setOverlaps((o) => (o.length ? [] : o))` alongside each `setGuides` in the resize cleanup branches.

newFindings.title: zero-gap DistributionGuide renders degenerate connector and "0" pill
newFindings.summary: `bestDistribution` uses `refGap >= 0` (Case B, line 242) and `free >= 0` (Case A, line 216) guards that allow a gap of exactly zero. When neighboring boards are touching flush, a `DistributionGuide` with `distance: 0` is produced. `projectGapGuide` computes `half = 0`, collapsing the connector to a zero-length segment (`ax === bx`). `AlignmentGuides.tsx` renders it with two coincident tick marks and a pill showing `Math.round(0) = "0"` — a floating orphan label with no visual connector. The fix is to change both guards to `> 0`; flush-touching boards are already handled by the `align` guide (edge-on-edge snap).

newFindings.title: Case B end-of-row distribution fires when dragged board still overlaps the rightmost neighbor
newFindings.summary: The Case B right-end guard at line 240 checks `dragCenter > b.origin + b.size / 2` (dragged center past the CENTER of the rightmost neighbor), not `dragCenter > b.origin + b.size` (dragged center past the FAR EDGE). When the dragged board's center is in the right half of `b` but the board still visually overlaps `b`, the condition fires and a rhythm-distribution snap to `b.right + refGap` is produced — semantically wrong since the user is not yet "beyond" b. In practice `consider()` usually rejects the target (diff > threshold), but the condition is inconsistent with the documented intent. The symmetric left-end check (`dragCenter < a2.origin + a2.size / 2`) has the same issue. Fix: use `b.origin + b.size` and `a2.origin` as the boundary.

newFindings.title: NoteCard grip registers document pointer-listeners with no unmount cleanup
newFindings.summary: NoteCard's grip onPointerDown registers three document event listeners (pointermove, pointerup, pointercancel) inline — outside any useEffect — with no corresponding cleanup on component unmount. The listeners are designed to self-remove inside the onUp callback once the user releases the pointer. If the NoteCard unmounts while a drag is in flight (board deleted, project switched, undo of note creation), onUp never runs and all three listeners remain permanently attached to document. Each dangling closure also holds references to the onDelete/note/ref props from the unmounted render, preventing GC. The leak accumulates across a session with frequent create-drag-delete cycles, adding unnecessary per-pointer-move overhead. Fix: store listener references in a ref and add a useEffect cleanup that removes them on unmount.

newFindings.title: FreeText grip registers document pointer-listeners with no unmount cleanup
newFindings.summary: FreeText has the identical listener-leak pattern as NoteCard (NEW-LEAK-1): the left-edge drag grip onPointerDown registers document.addEventListener for pointermove, pointerup, and pointercancel outside any useEffect, removed only in the onUp closure. If the FreeText element or its parent planning board unmounts mid-drag (delete, project switch, undo), the three listeners persist on document indefinitely — until the user releases the pointer. The fix is symmetric with NEW-LEAK-1: track the registered listeners in a ref and disconnect them in a useEffect cleanup.

newFindings.title: Keyboard-delete of the full-view board leaves fullViewEntering/fullViewClosing stale
newFindings.summary: The onNodesChange remove path (keyboard Backspace/Delete) calls removeBoard but not hardCloseFullView(). The boards effect later clears fullViewId but not fullViewClosing/fullViewEntering. Stale motion flags cause fullViewMotion to stay true, which defers Browser native-view attach on the next full-view open via BrowserPreviewLayer's motion guard. The boardActions.remove path correctly guards with if (fullViewIdRef.current === id) hardCloseFullView() — the keyboard path is the missing parity.

newFindings.title: Opening full view during the exit animation sets fullViewEntering=true with no settle timer — Browser native view stays detached forever
newFindings.summary: FullViewModal schedules setTimeout(onEntered, CAMERA_MS+16) in an effect with [onEntered] as its only dependency. onEntered (handleFullViewEntered) is a stable useCallback(...,[]) identity, so the enter effect fires only once — on initial modal mount. If the user opens full view, the animation settles, they start closing (fullViewClosing=true), and within 216ms reopen full view, openFullView sets fullViewClosing=false (cancelling the exit timer) and fullViewEntering=true. Since FullViewModal never unmounted, the enter effect does not re-fire and no new settle timer is scheduled. fullViewEntering stays true permanently. fullViewMotion = fullViewEntering || fullViewClosing stays true forever. BrowserPreviewLayer's rAF tick is gated on !fullViewMotionRef.current (line 673), so the Browser board's native view is never attached — the full-view modal shows only the stale snapshot.

newFindings.title: boardActions.duplicate unconditionally calls hardCloseFullView — exits full view when duplicating any board, not just the full-view one
newFindings.summary: The boardActions.duplicate handler (Canvas.tsx:385-388) calls hardCloseFullView() unconditionally before duplicateBoard(id), with no guard on whether id === fullViewIdRef.current. This means duplicating any board while a different board is in full view force-closes the full view unexpectedly. By contrast, boardActions.remove at lines 389-394 correctly guards with if (fullViewIdRef.current === id) hardCloseFullView(). The asymmetry is a user-facing defect: opening Board A in full view then duplicating Board B (via its context menu) dismisses Board A's full view with no warning.

newFindings.title: Rapid sequential openFullView calls share the original enter-settle timer — new board's fullViewEntering cleared prematurely at old board's animation offset
newFindings.summary: FullViewModal's enter effect (dep [onEntered], stable) fires only on mount. If the user opens Board A in full view (timer T1 at T+216ms), then — without closing — immediately opens Board B (via requestFullView on B, which calls openFullView(B) when fullViewIdRef.current !== B), FullViewModal stays mounted and the enter effect does not re-fire. fullViewEntering is set to true for Board B, but T1 fires at T+216ms from when Board A was opened, calling setFullViewEntering(false) after only (216 - elapsed) ms of Board B's animation. For a Browser board in full view, this premature fullViewMotion=false causes BrowserPreviewLayer to begin attaching the native view while the modal CSS transition may still be running, potentially binding to a mid-scale rect. Reachable via boardActions.requestFullView when a different board's maximize is clicked while another is in full view.

newFindings.title: electron-updater declared as a runtime dependency but autoUpdater is never initialized
newFindings.summary: electron-updater is in package.json 'dependencies' (packed into every release build) but autoUpdater is never imported or called anywhere in the source. When Phase 5 wires up the publish: feed in electron-builder.yml, the app will silently never check for updates because no code calls autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify(). The module is inert dead weight in every build until that integration is added. Currently harmless because publish: null means no update feed exists.

newFindings.title: FullViewModal has no focus trap — Tab exits the modal into background canvas
newFindings.summary: The FullViewModal renders a full-screen overlay but has no Tab-key focus trap, no inert attribute on background content, and no sentinel elements. Keyboard users can Tab past the scrim into canvas controls that are visually hidden behind the 66%-black overlay. The ARIA modal pattern requires focus to be confined to the dialog while it is open.

newFindings.title: FullViewModal does not restore focus to the triggering element on close
newFindings.summary: When the full-view modal closes via Esc, scrim click, or the maximize toggle, focus is not returned to the element that opened it. openFullView and closeFullView/hardCloseFullView contain no code to save or restore document.activeElement. After close, focus is lost to the body, leaving keyboard and screen-reader users stranded.

newFindings.title: role='menu' containers have no role='menuitem' on children — invalid ARIA
newFindings.summary: Both the board ⋯ overflow menu (BoardFrame.tsx:251) and the project-switcher dropdown (AppChrome.tsx:109) use role='menu' on their container div, but the interactive <button> children inside them have no role='menuitem'. The WAI-ARIA spec for the menu role requires menu-item-role descendants; without this, AT cannot identify individual items or apply menu keyboard navigation patterns.

newFindings.title: BoardMenu and ProjectSwitcher triggers missing aria-expanded / aria-haspopup
newFindings.summary: The ⋯ overflow IconBtn (BoardFrame.tsx:237-245) and the project-switcher trigger button (AppChrome.tsx:89-94) have no aria-expanded or aria-haspopup. The active prop on IconBtn is visual-only; the open state variable in ProjectSwitcher is never reflected on the button element. Screen-reader users have no indication that activating these controls opens a menu or what the current open/closed state is.

newFindings.title: TerminalConfig popover opens without moving keyboard focus into it
newFindings.summary: TerminalConfig renders with tabIndex={-1} on its container div but never calls .focus() on mount. Focus remains on the settings button inside the terminal area where xterm's stopPropagation on keydown intercepts all subsequent keystrokes. Keyboard users cannot Tab into the popover form, and Esc (defined in the popover's onKeyDown) is unreachable because xterm has captured focus. No role='dialog' or aria-label is set.

newFindings.title: Checklist toggle button exposes no machine-readable checked state (aria-checked missing)
newFindings.summary: Each checklist item toggle button (ChecklistCard.tsx:233-255) communicates done/undone state only through the title attribute wording and through color/icon inside the Checkbox <span> child. No aria-checked or aria-pressed attribute is set. Screen readers cannot announce the current state; the visual Checkbox is a plain <span> with color-only signals (filled blue background / transparent), violating WCAG 1.4.1.

newFindings.title: Checklist progress bar transition not gated on prefersReducedMotion()
newFindings.summary: The checklist progress bar fill uses an unconditional inline transition: 'width .18s' (ChecklistCard.tsx:222) that is not suppressed under prefers-reduced-motion. The CSS @media block in index.css only suppresses keyframe animations, not this inline transition. BoardFrame.tsx:394-396 correctly gates its box-shadow transition using prefersReducedMotion() — ChecklistCard lacks the same guard.

newFindings.title: FullViewModal missing role='dialog' and aria-modal — not announced as a dialog by AT
newFindings.summary: The fullview-scrim and fullview-frame divs (FullViewModal.tsx:68-82) carry no role='dialog', aria-modal='true', or aria-label. AT does not announce the overlay as a modal dialog when it opens, and does not restrict virtual-cursor navigation to the dialog's content. Background canvas content remains reachable via AT virtual cursor navigation because aria-modal is absent.

newFindings.title: Checklist drag permanently over-grows the planning board to the maximum mid-drag extent
newFindings.summary: ChecklistCard's onMeasureBottom useEffect has element.y as a dependency, so it fires on every pointer-move frame during a drag. PlanningBoard passes the translateElement-shifted viewElements to ChecklistCard, so element.y changes on every frame. When the user drags a checklist card down then back up within one gesture, onMeasureBottom is called with the peak (downward) y-position, triggering growBoardHeight with a larger needed value than the final committed position. Because growBoardHeight only-grows and never shrinks, the board remains permanently taller than the committed content requires.

newFindings.title: BrowserBoard URL bar silently rejects bare hostnames, showing 'Couldn't load' with no guidance
newFindings.summary: commitUrl in BrowserBoard stores any non-empty string verbatim. When a user types a bare host:port like localhost:5173 (without http://), the WHATWG URL parser interprets localhost: as a custom protocol, which isAllowedPreviewUrl on the main side rejects (only http: and https: are allowed). The main process emits did-fail-load and the board shows 'Couldn't load' with no explanation that prepending http:// would fix the problem. Confirmed: new URL('localhost:5173').protocol === 'localhost:'.

newFindings.title: ChecklistCard item onKeyDown has no interactive guard — Enter/Backspace mutate list when a non-select tool is active
newFindings.summary: The onKeyDown handler on each checklist item input calls onAddItem (Enter) and onRemoveItem (Backspace-on-empty) without checking the interactive prop. The input is readOnly when interactive=false, which prevents typing, but readOnly does not suppress keyDown events. If a checklist item input retains focus while the user switches to a non-select tool (arrow, pen, note, check), pressing Enter still appends a new item and Backspace on an empty item still removes it — mutating the checklist in a mode where element interaction is supposed to be disabled.
